title: Pattern: Deploy resources with a policy definition - Azure Policy
display_title: Deploy resources with deployIfNotExists policy
description: This Azure Policy pattern provides an example of how to deploy resources with a deployIfNotExists policy definition.
summary:
ThedeployIfNotExistseffect makes it possible to deploy
anAzure Resource Manager template(ARM
template) when creating or updating a resource that isn't compliant. This approach can be preferred
to using thedenyeffect as it lets resources continue to be created,
but ensures the changes are made to make them compliant.
classification_reason: Provides concrete deployIfNotExists Azure Policy definition patterns integrating ARM templates, including effect usage and structure, which are product-specific config/code patterns.

title: Pattern: Group policy definitions with initiatives - Azure Policy
display_title: Group Azure Policy definitions into initiatives
description: This Azure Policy pattern provides an example of how to group policy definitions into an initiative.
summary:
An initiative is a group of policy definitions. By grouping related policy definitions into a single
object, you can create a single assignment that would have been multiple assignments.
classification_reason: Shows exact structure and fields for initiatives (policySetDefinition) in Azure Policy JSON, which is product-specific configuration syntax.

title: Pattern: Logical operators in a policy definition - Azure Policy
display_title: Use logical operators in Azure Policy definitions
description: This Azure Policy pattern provides examples of how to use the logical operators in a policy definition.
summary:
A policy definition can contain several conditional statements. You might need each statement to be
true or only need some of them to be true. To support these needs, the language haslogical operatorsfornot,allOf,
andanyOf. They're optional and can be nested to create complex scenarios.
classification_reason: Shows concrete Azure Policy JSON patterns using not, allOf, anyOf with real schema fields and structure. These are product-specific policy language patterns and configuration details rather than generic logic concepts.

title: Pattern: Parameters in a policy definition - Azure Policy
display_title: Define and use parameters in Azure Policy definitions
description: This Azure Policy pattern provides an example of how to use string and array parameters in a policy definition, and how to parameterize the effect.
summary:
A policy definition can be made dynamic to reduce the number of policy definitions that are needed
by usingparameters. The parameter is defined
during policy assignment. Parameters have a set of pre-defined properties that describe the
parameter and how it's used.
classification_reason: Shows concrete JSON for string/array parameters, parameter properties, and effect parameterization in Azure Policy. These are specific to the Azure Policy definition schema and not generic programming parameters.

title: Pattern: Using tags in a policy definition - Azure Policy

managing, organizing, and governing your Azure resources. Azure Policy makes it possible to
configure tags on your new and existing resources at scale with themodifyeffect andremediation tasks.
classification_reason: Provides concrete Azure Policy JSON patterns for parameterized and inherited tags using modify/remediation; these are product-specific code/config patterns rather than generic concepts.

title: Pattern: The value operator in a policy definition - Azure Policy

Thevalueoperator evaluatesparameters,supported template functions, or literals
to a provided value for a givencondition. Warning If the result of atemplate functionis an error, policy evaluation fails. A failed evaluation

title: Tutorial: Build policies to enforce compliance - Azure Policy
description: In this tutorial, you use policies to enforce standards, control costs, maintain security, and impose enterprise-wide design principles.
summary:
Understanding how to create and manage policies in Azure is important for staying compliant with
your corporate standards and service-level agreements. In this tutorial, you learn to use Azure
Policy to do some of the more common tasks related to creating, assigning, and managing policies
